edges.description: KMT2D/KDM6A haploinsufficiency reduces enhancer H3K4me1 deposition and H3K27me3 demethylation, dysregulating developmental enhancers required for neural crest specification and migration.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: Reduced KMT2D activity in cardiomyocytes lowers H3K4me1/me2 at enhancers controlling ion-transport and cell-cycle programs.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: KMT2D/KDM6A loss disrupts H3K4me1 deposition at SOX9/RUNX2 enhancers controlling chondrogenic differentiation and growth-plate hypertrophy.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: KMT2D loss alters enhancer activity at immune loci, including class-switch and integrin genes, impairing terminal B-cell maturation.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: Reduced KMT2D activity decreases dentate gyrus H3K4me3 and impairs adult hippocampal neurogenesis.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: KMT2D/KDM6A loss in pancreatic beta cells perturbs insulin secretion regulation, predisposing to congenital hyperinsulinism.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: KMT2D loss in cochlear hair cells contributes to sensorineural hearing loss independent of structural ear malformation.
edges.source: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

edges.description: Disrupted cranial neural crest patterning produces the elongated palpebral fissure phenotype.
Altered cranial neural crest morphogenesis contributes to the characteristic eyebrow gestalt.
KMT2D loss in neural crest impairs secondary palatal shelf elevation and ECM expression, producing cleft palate.
Persistent fetal fingertip pads are part of the broader neurocristopathy phenotype but specific molecular intermediates are not fully defined.
Cardiac neural crest dysfunction contributes to outflow-tract and left-sided obstructive lesions.
intermediate_mechanisms: Impaired cardiac neural crest migration into outflow tract
Notch pathway dysregulation during endocardial patterning

edges.description: Impaired terminal B-cell maturation reduces class-switched IgA-producing plasma cells.
Hypogammaglobulinemia and impaired class switching predispose to recurrent sinopulmonary and otologic infections.
B-cell tolerance and selection defects predispose a subset of patients to autoimmune cytopenias.
Reduced adult hippocampal neurogenesis contributes to learning and memory deficits underlying intellectual disability.
Neurodevelopmental chromatin disruption affecting postnatal neurogenesis contributes to delayed speech and language development beyond purely structural (hearing/oromotor) causes.
Neuron-specific chromatin disruption may predispose a subset of patients to seizures; precise epileptogenic mechanism in Kabuki syndrome is not fully defined.
KMT2D-dependent enhancer activation in cardiomyocytes is required for normal cardiac gene expression; loss produces septal and outflow-tract defects.
intermediate_mechanisms: Dysregulated ion transport and cell-cycle gene expression

edges.description: Premature/dysregulated chondrocyte differentiation and shortened growth-plate hypertrophic zones contribute to postnatal growth deficiency.
Cranial-base chondrocyte differentiation defects impair endochondral ossification and palatal shelf elevation.
Generalized chondrocyte differentiation defects contribute to skeletal anomalies including scoliosis.
Dysregulated insulin secretion in KMT2D/KDM6A-deficient beta cells produces persistent neonatal hypoglycemia.
KMT2D loss in cochlear hair cells produces cell-intrinsic outer hair cell dysfunction underlying sensorineural hearing loss.
Combined sensorineural (hair cell) and conductive (recurrent otitis media) components produce the overall Kabuki hearing impairment phenotype.

edges.target: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

nodes.description: Arched and broad eyebrows with lateral third notching or sparseness are a core component of the characteristic Kabuki facial gestalt.

evidence.reference_title: Kabuki syndrome: international consensus diagnostic criteria.
evidence.snippet: Typical dysmorphic features include long palpebral fissures with eversion of the lateral third of the lower eyelid and two or more of the following: (1) arched and broad eyebrows with the lateral third displaying notching or sparseness; (2) short columella with depressed nasal tip; (3) large, prominent or cupped ears; and (4) persistent fingertip pads.
HP:0002553 | Highly arched eyebrow | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: A subset of patients develop autoimmune manifestations including immune thrombocytopenic purpura, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and vitiligo, reflecting the paradox of concurrent immunodeficiency and immune dysregulation. Registry data quantify autoimmune disease in 13.6% overall and 25.6% of adults.

evidence.reference_title: Immunopathological manifestations in Kabuki syndrome: a registry study of 177 individuals.
evidence.snippet: 13.6% (24/177) had autoimmune disease

evidence.reference_title: Immunopathological manifestations in Kabuki syndrome: a registry study of 177 individuals.
evidence.snippet: The most frequent AID manifestations were immune thrombocytopenic purpura

evidence.reference_title: Immunopathological manifestations in Kabuki syndrome: a registry study of 177 individuals.
evidence.snippet: Among nonhematological manifestations, vitiligo was frequent.

nodes.description: KMT2D deficiency impairs terminal B-cell maturation, class-switched memory B-cell formation, and humoral immune competence. This creates a clinically important immune phenotype with hypogammaglobulinemia, IgA deficiency, recurrent infections, and in a subset of patients autoimmune disease.

evidence.reference_title: Defects of B-cell terminal differentiation in patients with type-1 Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Among the patients with KMT2D mutations (KMT2D(Mut/+)), hypogammaglobulinemia was detected in all but 1 patient, with IgA deficiency affecting 90% of patients and a deficiency in at least 1 other isoform seen in 40% of patients.

evidence.reference_title: Defects of B-cell terminal differentiation in patients with type-1 Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Impaired terminal differentiation was noted in primary B cells from patients with KMT2D(Mut/+) mutations.

evidence.reference_title: Immunopathological manifestations in Kabuki syndrome: a registry study of 177 individuals.
evidence.snippet: Overall, 44.1% (78/177) and 58.2% (46/79) of KS individuals exhibited infection susceptibility and hypogammaglobulinemia, respectively;

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D regulates activation, localization, and integrin expression by T-cells.
evidence.snippet: we reveal decreased expression (both at the transcriptional and translational levels) of a cluster of leukocyte-specific integrins, which perturb aspects of T-cell activation, maturation, adhesion/localization, and effector function.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D regulates activation, localization, and integrin expression by T-cells.
evidence.snippet: H3K4me3 ChIP-PCR suggests that these evolutionary similar integrins are under direct control of KMT2D.

nodes.description: Surgical repair of congenital heart defects including septal defects, coarctation of the aorta, and other structural cardiac anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital heart defects in Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: CHD was diagnosed in 35 (58%) of our patients. Aortic coarctation (COA) (23%), atrial septal defect (ASD) (20%), and ventricular septal defect (VSD) (17%) were the most frequent CHDs

nodes.description: Beyond a neural-crest contribution, KMT2D acts cell-autonomously in cardiac precursors and cardiomyocytes during cardiogenesis. Myocardial Kmt2d deletion in mice reduces enhancer/promoter H3K4me1 and H3K4me2, downregulates ion-transport and cell-cycle genes, and produces altered calcium handling. Zebrafish models additionally implicate Notch dysregulation during endocardial patterning, contributing to congenital heart disease.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D regulates specific programs in heart development via histone H3 lysine 4 di-methylation.
evidence.snippet: we demonstrate a requirement for KMT2D in cardiac precursors and cardiomyocytes during cardiogenesis in mice.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D regulates specific programs in heart development via histone H3 lysine 4 di-methylation.
evidence.snippet: myocardial Kmt2d deletion led to decreased H3K4me1 and H3K4me2 at enhancers and promoters.

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of Notch signaling rescues cardiovascular development in Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Our zebrafish Kabuki Syndrome model reveals a regulatory link between the Notch pathway and Kmt2d during endothelium and endocardium patterning

nodes.description: KMT2D is required for hypertrophic chondrocyte differentiation and endochondral ossification. KS1 and KS2 cellular models exhibit precocious chondrocyte differentiation, shortened growth-plate hypertrophic zones, and convergent transcriptomic dysregulation downstream of SOX9 and RUNX2 enhancer programs. This contributes to postnatal growth deficiency, skeletal anomalies, and cleft palate via cranial-base ossification defects.

evidence.reference_title: Growth deficiency in a mouse model of Kabuki syndrome 2 bears mechanistic similarities to Kabuki syndrome 1.
evidence.snippet: Kdm6atm1d/+ growth plates are also shorter, due to decreases in hypertrophic chondrocyte size and hypertrophic zone height.

evidence.reference_title: Growth deficiency in a mouse model of Kabuki syndrome 2 bears mechanistic similarities to Kabuki syndrome 1.
evidence.snippet: RNA-seq simultaneously on Kmt2d-/-, Kdm6a-/-, and control lines at Days 7 and 14 of differentiation. This revealed surprising resemblance in gene expression between Kmt2d-/- and Kdm6a-/-

evidence.reference_title: The KMT2D Kabuki syndrome histone methylase controls neural crest cell differentiation and facial morphology.
evidence.snippet: KMT2D mutant chondrocytes in the cranial base fail to properly differentiate, leading to defective endochondral ossification.

nodes.description: Kabuki syndrome is a disorder of epigenetic regulation in which loss of KMT2D or KDM6A function perturbs COMPASS-associated chromatin control. Truncating KMT2D variants frequently undergo nonsense-mediated decay, reducing KMT2D dosage and dysregulating developmental target genes. KDM6A lesions disrupt a cooperating histone demethylase in the same chromatin-regulatory pathway.
nodes.id: Chromatin dysregulation from KMT2D and KDM6A haploinsufficiency

evidence.reference_title: Molecular analysis, pathogenic mechanisms, and readthrough therapy on a large cohort of Kabuki syndrome patients.
evidence.snippet: Kabuki syndrome (KS) is a multiple congenital anomalies syndrome characterized by characteristic facial features and varying degrees of mental retardation, caused by mutations in KMT2D/MLL2 and KDM6A/UTX genes.

evidence.reference_title: Molecular analysis, pathogenic mechanisms, and readthrough therapy on a large cohort of Kabuki syndrome patients.
evidence.snippet: We found that a number of KMT2D truncating mutations result in mRNA degradation through the nonsense-mediated mRNA decay, contributing to protein haploinsufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Deletion of KDM6A, a histone demethylase interacting with MLL2, in three patients with Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Here, we describe three KS individuals with de novo partial or complete deletions of an X chromosome gene, KDM6A, that encodes a histone demethylase that interacts with MLL2.

evidence.reference_title: Characterizing the molecular impact of KMT2D variants on the epigenetic and transcriptional landscapes in Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Our analysis identified unique enhancer signatures in H3K4me1 and H3K4me2 in KS compared with controls.

evidence.reference_title: Characterizing the molecular impact of KMT2D variants on the epigenetic and transcriptional landscapes in Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: 31% of super-enhancers of normal blood cells overlapped with disrupted enhancers in KS, supporting an association of reduced enhancer activity of immune-related genes with immune deficiency phenotypes.

evidence.reference_title: Histone H3 lysine 4 methyltransferase KMT2D.
evidence.snippet: KMT2D is a major mammalian H3K4 mono-methyltransferase and co-localizes with lineage determining transcription factors on transcriptional enhancers. It is required for the binding of histone H3K27 acetyltransferases CBP and p300 on enhancers, enhancer activation and cell-type specific gene expression during differentiation.

nodes.description: Cleft palate occurs in a subset of patients, consistent with the neurocristopathy and chondrocyte-differentiation mechanisms. Reported at 26.1% in a Taiwanese cohort. Mouse models with conditional KMT2D loss in neural crest exhibit fully penetrant cleft palate driven by defective secondary palatal shelf elevation and cranial-base ossification deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and Phenotypic Spectrum of KMT2D Variants in Taiwanese Case Series of Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Other abnormalities included hearing loss (39.1%), seizures (26.1%), cleft palate (26.1%), and renal anomalies (21.7%).

evidence.reference_title: The KMT2D Kabuki syndrome histone methylase controls neural crest cell differentiation and facial morphology.
evidence.snippet: KMT2D mutant NCCs lead to defective secondary palatal shelf elevation with reduced expression of extracellular matrix components.
HP:0000175 | Cleft palate | Frequent (79-30%)

evidence.reference_title: Cleft palate in Kabuki syndrome: a report of six cases.
evidence.snippet: Although cleft palate is a feature that is sometimes observed in patients with Kabuki syndrome, there are few clinical reports of cleft palate associated with Kabuki syndrome.

nodes.description: KMT2D loss causes cell-intrinsic cochlear outer hair cell dysfunction, contributing to sensorineural hearing loss in addition to the conductive component from recurrent otitis media. Mouse models show diminished distortion product otoacoustic emissions despite no gross cochlear structural malformation on micro-CT.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D Deficiency Causes Sensorineural Hearing Loss in Mice and Humans.
evidence.snippet: The KS1 mice also display diminished distortion product otoacoustic emission levels, which suggests outer hair cell dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D Deficiency Causes Sensorineural Hearing Loss in Mice and Humans.
evidence.snippet: our data suggests that KMT2D dysfunction causes sensorineural hearing loss compounded with external factors, such as infection.

nodes.description: Congenital heart disease is common in Kabuki syndrome, especially left-sided obstructive lesions and septal defects. Detected in 70% of KMT2D-positive patients in one cohort and 69.6% in a Taiwanese 23-patient series.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital heart defects in molecularly proven Kabuki syndrome patients.
evidence.snippet: In conclusion, a CHD is detected in 70% of patients with KMT2D (MLL2) pathogenic variants, most commonly left-sided obstructive lesions, including multiple left-sided obstructions similar to those observed in the spectrum of the Shone complex, and septal defects.
HP:0001627 | Abnormal heart morphology | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Humoral immune dysfunction often includes decreased serum IgA levels with broader hypogammaglobulinemia in a subset of patients. IgA deficiency affects 90% of KMT2D-positive patients in a French cohort, and hypogammaglobulinemia 58.2% in a 177-patient registry.

evidence.reference_title: Defects of B-cell terminal differentiation in patients with type-1 Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Among the patients with KMT2D mutations (KMT2D(Mut/+)), hypogammaglobulinemia was detected in all but 1 patient, with IgA deficiency affecting 90% of patients and a deficiency in at least 1 other isoform seen in 40% of patients.
HP:0002720 | Decreased circulating IgA level | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: First-line pharmacotherapy for hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia in Kabuki syndrome. Activates pancreatic beta-cell K-ATP channels to suppress insulin secretion. Approximately 92% of children with KS-associated hyperinsulinism respond to a diazoxide trial.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and Molecular Characterization of Hyperinsulinism in Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: HI treatment was discontinued in 46% of the cohort at median age 2.8 years

nodes.description: Growth hormone replacement may be considered for patients with documented growth hormone deficiency contributing to postnatal growth retardation.

evidence.reference_title: Growth Hormone Therapy in Children with Kabuki Syndrome: 1-year Treatment Results.
evidence.snippet: All participants experienced catch-up growth during the year of rhGH treatment, but without an influence on body proportions.

nodes.description: Histone deacetylase inhibitors such as AR-42 have shown preclinical promise in rescuing structural and functional brain deficits in Kmt2d haploinsufficient mouse models. By promoting histone acetylation, HDAC inhibitors can partially compensate for the reduced H3K4 methylation caused by KMT2D deficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Histone deacetylase inhibition rescues structural and functional brain deficits in a mouse model of Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: These abnormalities improved upon postnatal treatment with AR-42. Our work suggests that a reversible deficiency in postnatal neurogenesis underlies intellectual disability in Kabuki syndrome.

nodes.description: Hearing loss is common in Kabuki syndrome and combines conductive components (driven by recurrent otitis media) with cell-intrinsic sensorineural components (cochlear outer hair cell dysfunction). Mean age of presentation is approximately 7 years; reported in 39.1% of a Taiwanese cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Kidney and urinary tract findings among patients with Kabuki (make-up) syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Kabuki syndrome (KS) is a genetic disorder caused mainly by de novo pathogenic variants in KMT2D or KDM6A, characterized by recognizable facial features, intellectual disability, and multi-systemic involvement, including short stature, microcephaly, hearing loss, cardiac defects, and additional congenital anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Developmental outcome in Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The presence or absence of hearing loss or major malformations, other than those involving the brain, was not predictive of developmental outcome.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D Deficiency Causes Sensorineural Hearing Loss in Mice and Humans.
evidence.snippet: individuals have both sensorineural and conductive hearing loss, with the average age of presentation being 7 years.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and Phenotypic Spectrum of KMT2D Variants in Taiwanese Case Series of Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Other abnormalities included hearing loss (39.1%), seizures (26.1%), cleft palate (26.1%), and renal anomalies (21.7%).
HP:0000405 | Conductive hearing impairment | Frequent (79-30%)
HP:0000407 | Sensorineural hearing impairment | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Congenital hyperinsulinism causing persistent neonatal hypoglycemia is a recognized presenting feature of Kabuki syndrome. Reported in both KMT2D and KDM6A patients, frequently diazoxide-responsive.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hyperinsulinism as the presenting feature of Kabuki syndrome: clinical and molecular characterization of 9 affected individuals.
evidence.snippet: The incidence of HI among patients with KS may be higher than previously reported, and KS may account for as much as 1% of patients diagnosed with HI.
HP:0000825 | Hyperinsulinemic hypoglycemia | Very rare (<4-1%)

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and Molecular Characterization of Hyperinsulinism in Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Hypoglycemia was recognized on the first day of life in 25 children (76%).

nodes.description: Intravenous or subcutaneous immunoglobulin replacement therapy for patients with severe hypogammaglobulinemia and recurrent infections.

evidence.reference_title: Immune abnormalities are a frequent manifestation of Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Due to this increased susceptibility to infection, children with KS should have immunologic evaluations at the time of diagnosis in order to reduce preventable morbidity and mortality.

nodes.description: Developmental delay and usually mild-to-moderate intellectual disability are central neurodevelopmental manifestations.

evidence.reference_title: Kabuki syndrome: international consensus diagnostic criteria.
evidence.snippet: The authors propose that a definitive diagnosis can be made in an individual of any age with a history of infantile hypotonia, developmental delay and/or intellectual disability, and one or both of the following major criteria: (1) a pathogenic or likely pathogenic variant in KMT2D or KDM6A; and (2) typical dysmorphic features (defined below) at some point of life.

evidence.reference_title: Developmental outcome in Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Based on these patients and a review of the literature, in the absence of major structural brain anomalies, the average intelligence quotient (IQ) in patients with this condition fall within the mild mental retardation range, however, specific developmental outcomes are widely variable, ranging from severe MR to normal intelligence.
HP:0001249 | Intellectual disability | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Lysine-specific demethylase 1A (KDM1A/LSD1) normally removes H3K4 methyl marks added by KMT2D. Pharmacologic KDM1A inhibition (e.g., TAK-418) rebalances H3K4 methylation, restores postnatal neurogenesis, and rescues hippocampal memory in Kmt2d-haploinsufficient mice. Targets the upstream chromatin defect more directly than HDAC inhibition.

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of KDM1A activity restores adult neurogenesis and improves hippocampal memory in a mouse model of Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: our data suggest that KDM1A inhibition is a plausible treatment strategy for KS

nodes.description: Long palpebral fissures with eversion of the lateral lower eyelid are a core component of the characteristic Kabuki facial gestalt.

evidence.reference_title: Kabuki syndrome: international consensus diagnostic criteria.
evidence.snippet: Typical dysmorphic features include long palpebral fissures with eversion of the lateral third of the lower eyelid and two or more of the following: (1) arched and broad eyebrows with the lateral third displaying notching or sparseness; (2) short columella with depressed nasal tip; (3) large, prominent or cupped ears; and (4) persistent fingertip pads.
HP:0000637 | Long palpebral fissure | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Ketogenic/Modified Atkins dietary intervention is being investigated as a chromatin-modulating therapy in Kabuki syndrome. Multi-omics data show partial reversal of ribosomal protein dysregulation in a treated KMT2D KS patient. A completed 12-week adult phase-1 trial (NCT04722315) evaluated cognitive and methylation outcomes.

Experimental models indicate that Kabuki syndrome is a neurocristopathy. Impaired chromatin regulation in neural crest derivatives disrupts craniofacial morphogenesis, left-sided cardiovascular development, and postnatal growth, helping explain the characteristic facial gestalt, congenital heart disease, and growth deficiency seen clinically.

biological_processes: neural crest cell migration involved in heart formation

evidence.reference_title: UTX-guided neural crest function underlies craniofacial features of Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: We now establish Kabuki syndrome as a neurocristopathy, whereby the majority of clinical features are modeled in mice carrying neural crest (NC) deletion of UTX, including craniofacial dysmorphism, cardiac defects, and postnatal growth retardation.

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of Notch signaling rescues cardiovascular development in Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Our zebrafish Kabuki Syndrome model reveals a regulatory link between the Notch pathway and Kmt2d during endothelium and endocardium patterning and shows that pharmacological inhibition of Notch signaling rebalances Rbpj protein levels and rescues the cardiovascular phenotype by enhancing endothelial and endocardial cell proliferation and stabilizing endocardial patterning.

evidence.reference_title: The KMT2D Kabuki syndrome histone methylase controls neural crest cell differentiation and facial morphology.
evidence.snippet: KMT2D NCC knockout mice demonstrate hypoplasia with reductions in frontonasal bone lengths.

evidence.reference_title: The KMT2D Kabuki syndrome histone methylase controls neural crest cell differentiation and facial morphology.
evidence.snippet: KMT2D NCC loss-of-function does exhibit unique phenotypes distinct from UTX mutation, including fully penetrant cleft palate, mandible hypoplasia and deficits in cranial base ossification.

evidence.reference_title: The histone methyltransferase KMT2D, mutated in Kabuki syndrome patients, is required for neural crest cell formation and migration.
evidence.snippet: Kmt2d knockdown correlates with a decrease in H3K4 monomethylation and H3K27 acetylation supporting a role of Kmt2d in the transcriptional activation of target genes.

evidence.reference_title: The histone methyltransferase KMT2D, mutated in Kabuki syndrome patients, is required for neural crest cell formation and migration.
evidence.snippet: Kmt2d loss-of-function inhibits Xenopus Sema3F expression, and overexpression of Sema3F can partially rescue Kmt2d loss-of-function defects.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D deficiency leads to cellular developmental disorders and enhancer dysregulation in neural-crest-containing brain organoids.
evidence.snippet: GREE identified that KMT2D targets a roof-plate-like niche cell and activates the niche cell-specific WNT3A enhancer, providing the microenvironment for neural crest and neuronal development.

nodes.description: Pathogenic variants in KMT2D and KDM6A predispose to congenital hyperinsulinism through dysregulated insulin secretion in pancreatic beta cells. Hypoglycemia frequently presents in the neonatal period and is typically diazoxide-responsive.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital hyperinsulinism as the presenting feature of Kabuki syndrome: clinical and molecular characterization of 9 affected individuals.
evidence.snippet: Molecular diagnoses of KS were established by identification of pathogenic variants in KMT2D (n = 5) and KDM6A (n = 4).

evidence.reference_title: Clinical and Molecular Characterization of Hyperinsulinism in Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Diazoxide trial was conducted in 25 children, 92% of whom were responsive.

nodes.description: Animal data support a chromatin-based neurodevelopmental mechanism in which reduced KMT2D activity decreases dentate gyrus H3K4me3, impairs postnatal neurogenesis, and contributes to hippocampal-dependent memory deficits.

evidence.reference_title: Histone deacetylase inhibition rescues structural and functional brain deficits in a mouse model of Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: In vivo, deficiency of H3K4me3 in the dentate gyrus granule cell layer of Kmt2d(+/βGeo) mice correlated with reduced neurogenesis and hippocampal memory defects.

evidence.reference_title: Inhibition of KDM1A activity restores adult neurogenesis and improves hippocampal memory in a mouse model of Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: orally administered TAK-418 increases the numbers of newly born doublecortin (DCX)+ cells and processes in the hippocampus in a dose-dependent manner.

evidence.reference_title: Neuron-specific chromatin disruption at CpG islands and aging-related regions in Kabuki syndrome mice.
evidence.snippet: Neurons, but not B or T cells, show preferential chromatin disruption at CpG islands and at regulatory elements linked to aging.

nodes.description: Persistent fetal fingertip pads are a hallmark feature included in the consensus diagnostic criteria.

evidence.reference_title: Kabuki syndrome: international consensus diagnostic criteria.
evidence.snippet: Typical dysmorphic features include long palpebral fissures with eversion of the lateral third of the lower eyelid and two or more of the following: (1) arched and broad eyebrows with the lateral third displaying notching or sparseness; (2) short columella with depressed nasal tip; (3) large, prominent or cupped ears; and (4) persistent fingertip pads.
HP:0001212 | Prominent fingertip pads | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Recurrent sinopulmonary and otologic infections are common clinical consequences of the associated antibody deficiency. Reported in 44.1% of a 177-patient KS registry and 65.2% of a 23-patient Taiwanese cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Immunopathological manifestations in Kabuki syndrome: a registry study of 177 individuals.
evidence.snippet: Overall, 44.1% (78/177) and 58.2% (46/79) of KS individuals exhibited infection susceptibility and hypogammaglobulinemia, respectively;
HP:0002719 | Recurrent infections | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Skeletal anomalies including scoliosis are well-documented manifestations of Kabuki syndrome, linked to altered neural crest and chondrocyte development.

evidence.reference_title: Kabuki Syndrome-Clinical Review with Molecular Aspects.
evidence.snippet: hypertrichosis and scoliosis.

evidence.reference_title: Congenital heart defects in Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Kabuki makeup (Niikawa-Kuroki) syndrome (KS) is characterized by distinct facial anomalies, mental retardation, congenital heart defect (CHD), and skeletal malformations.
HP:0002650 | Scoliosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Seizures occur in a minority of patients with Kabuki syndrome, reported at 26.1% in a Taiwanese cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and Phenotypic Spectrum of KMT2D Variants in Taiwanese Case Series of Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Other abnormalities included hearing loss (39.1%), seizures (26.1%), cleft palate (26.1%), and renal anomalies (21.7%).
HP:0001250 | Seizure | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Sensorineural hearing loss is documented in Kabuki syndrome and arises from cochlear hair cell dysfunction independent of recurrent otitis media or structural ear anomalies. Mouse models show diminished distortion product otoacoustic emissions consistent with outer hair cell dysfunction.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D Deficiency Causes Sensorineural Hearing Loss in Mice and Humans.
evidence.snippet: individuals have both sensorineural and conductive hearing loss, with the average age of presentation being 7 years.

evidence.reference_title: KMT2D Deficiency Causes Sensorineural Hearing Loss in Mice and Humans.
evidence.snippet: The KS1 mice also display diminished distortion product otoacoustic emission levels, which suggests outer hair cell dysfunction.

nodes.description: Postnatal growth deficiency commonly leads to short stature. About 28% of KS children meet biochemical criteria for growth hormone deficiency, and growth-plate hypertrophic chondrocyte abnormalities are documented in both KS1 and KS2 mouse models.

evidence.reference_title: A mutation screen in patients with Kabuki syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Kabuki syndrome (KS) is one of the classical, clinically well-known multiple anomalies/mental retardation syndromes, mainly characterized by a very distinctive facial appearance in combination with additional clinical signs such as developmental delay, short stature, persistent fingerpads, and urogenital tract anomalies.

evidence.reference_title: Growth Hormone Stimulation Tests in Children with Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Five of the 18 children (27.8%) were biochemically GH deficient.
HP:0004322 | Short stature | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Delayed speech and language development is a near-universal feature of Kabuki syndrome, reported in 78.3% of a 23-patient Taiwanese cohort. Reflects both intellectual disability and the structural/oromotor contributions of cleft palate, hearing loss, and hypotonia.

evidence.reference_title: Genetic and Phenotypic Spectrum of KMT2D Variants in Taiwanese Case Series of Kabuki Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The most common clinical characteristics included distinct facial features (100%), intellectual disability (100%), developmental delay (95.7%), speech delay (78.3%), hypotonia (69.6%), congenital heart abnormalities (69.6%), and recurrent infections (65.2%).
HP:0012758 | Neurodevelopmental delay | Very frequent (99-80%)
